speaker: interviewer
text: --October 7, 1996, Irving Gromis. When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to [INAUDIBLE], which my son asked me documents, I sended them. And that was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you come in '47 to the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- yeah. But in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for America, we're talking about the beginning [INAUDIBLE], which I have-- I have paperwork. And I work in [INAUDIBLE] and actually in concentration-- [INAUDIBLE] concentration camp. [INAUDIBLE] camp they call it, was a [INAUDIBLE]. [? We talked to ?] other people and we feel comfortable. And from [INAUDIBLE], a big business, I go to Poland to bring here. One time I went to Poland, I came back and [INAUDIBLE] couple hundred dollars, couple hours. That much, $200, is a lot of money. The second time I went to Poland, I should come into home. My wife is in hospital. I came the second day. She in hospital. And this lady told me that story. She said, your wife is in hospital. He is a boy. I went at the hospital. There's only one hospital in [INAUDIBLE]. And they showed me the-- the boy to the window. And I speak to my wife, was couple hours sitting with her, sitting with, eating with her. And I came happy with the boy. And I didn't bring any money. I told the her, ah, don't use the money. [INAUDIBLE] life. And I feel very healthy. I don't want to walk with you. Then we went home in a rent apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Extra-- she didn't want to stay with the apartment. This costs money. She want to stay with the bubbe, with the zaide, grandfather all together. And this lady was living to the front, this lady. But she [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a woman who was here earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's who you're talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not this one, the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't come. And she comes in. And he talks, [INAUDIBLE], friendly each other? And saw it. And she has pictures. She should bring pictures. She has pictures from-- from there. I said, you come, and you bring the pictures. Or, they tell me, I told her not to come. But when they come in, I call her, comes. And she says to me, you know, I have apartment. I have everything. And she doesn't want to the apartment. She wants to have the father with her. We slept together on the floor. She said, I want to sleep in the floor with the mother, with the father. We slept in the floor all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: That couple of months, and I've been registered my name Rechman. No. Gromis is not. You didn't hear that I'll comes Rechman to Gromis. Didn't I tell you this story? You can say it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I will tell you. OK. Then I am Rechman.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, you didn't tell the story on tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I didn't-- I have to tell you the tape. OK, then. The changes from Rechman to Gromis-- you want to cut it up, or are you [? bolting ?] to 1946? What will you do? From Rehman to Gromis, didn't I tell you that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not on tape. No, not-- not on camera. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I got the death penalty in 1942 from the [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE]. The doc-- Mr. Winkler [INAUDIBLE] from the [INAUDIBLE]. He says, I can't to [INAUDIBLE]. I'll give you two weeks later, you're going to-- I'll hold you two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In two weeks, you have to get. The call the-- the-- the gypsy-- when they call the gypsies-- I tell you these gypsies? And they lead me in with the gypsies together. Yes. You talked about the gypsies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went out with the gypsies. I told you the why I said. I went out with the gypsies. And I was hiding in the roof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then [? Eichman, ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? Eichman, ?] was arrested in 1941. Then send him away Siberia. And they gave me a Polish passport for 1937, Polack, with a name [? Eichman-- ?] maybe he's living-- [? Eichman ?] Israel, Jacob [?Eichman?] and I [INAUDIBLE] passport. OK? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I came into [INAUDIBLE] to deliver-- to deliver the goods. I says, I need to bring to [INAUDIBLE]. And to the window, it was written-- I see in the-- the [INAUDIBLE] to go into the [INAUDIBLE] had written, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means Jewish dogs not allowed in. Well, see, I go away. Now, since I changed my name in the-- in through the window he calls me. Gromis, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He kill me. He recognize me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says to him, I'm not Gromis. I'm-- [INAUDIBLE], don't talk anything like this. [? Eichman-- ?] [? Eichman, ?] this is a extra German name. Don't use that name. OK? [? Eichman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: This [INAUDIBLE]. I came home. He talked to the president from the Judenrat. He told me use [? Rochman. ?] Now, put out from the E, make an R. From the E, I make a R in the Polish document. And I've been registered in the Judenrat [? Rochman, ?] not [? Eichman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the Polish certificate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm [? Rochman. ?] And since then I've been using [? Rochman. ?] I keep [INAUDIBLE] me I'll keep it [? Rochman. ?] It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back to Gromis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back to Gromis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Gromis in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to America, [? I came as ?] [? Rochman. ?] I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about when you came to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to America. I went to the America, came [? Rochman. ?] [INAUDIBLE] was in December 12, 1947 into America. I remember, because I said my son asked me the same thing. 1947 when I came over here. I couldn't find apartment. Suddenly, I did find apartment. I've been living Rivington-- 24 Rivington Street. 24 Rivington was 1948 already. 1948, my [INAUDIBLE] wife get there. She's pregnant and having a little boy. And it was critical. But the Joint gives me to $15, $20 a month. My wife, I could manage it. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint Distribution Committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Joint Distribution Committee, they gave me. And I, on the side, I was looking for work. I make a dollar extra, dollar here, pulling packages. Here, there, I make a $3, $4-- [INAUDIBLE] a dollar a day. It's enough, $2, $1.50. And the Joint give me the few dollars. And I rent apartment. I-- no, I give $200 already for the apartment. Years ago, it was in the [INAUDIBLE]. I got an apartment on Rivington Street, 24 Rivington Street near the matzoh bakery. I'm looking for a job. I can't find a job. Sign [INAUDIBLE] up, it's a job. He [INAUDIBLE] says, no Jewish. No refugees. You ought to go to Jewish. I went to Syracuse, New York. And she was living also down there. And I had a job down there, a Saturday, Sunday. Saturday, I work. They give me [INAUDIBLE] job in the synagogue. I read the Bible, read the Torah. I know how to read it. They give me a job in the religious-- ah, no he [INAUDIBLE]. Somebody should tell me this is no good, this is no good. I don't want that. I want to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the synagogue. They said, could you read the Torah? I read the Torah one [? time, ?] a second. And he give me a $1, dollar two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says to the rabbi, I don't want that [INAUDIBLE] business. They give me three and a half thousand-- $4,000 a year were not taxable payment with food, with everything. I don't want the job. And I make the job only less. I make $0.75 an hour to work overtime. I work another place, then I make $50 a week. A lot of money was that time, $60 a week, and that's it. With my wife, I could live. Because she managed everything exactly. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] job close to work, I bring home this [INAUDIBLE]. My wife make it a place. And she invited somebody [INAUDIBLE], this and this. And I had nice, very good. I work in Syracuse in the factory. Somebody works with me [INAUDIBLE] was bonded with me, was with me. I said only to the-- to the workers. And the boss says, Mr. [? Shreman ?] listen, don't bring up the European stories over here. Because I do not listen to it. You explain me to my best guy. He was a strong guy. Sure. I went to another factory. I make a $1.20 an hour. And [INAUDIBLE] says, now, [INAUDIBLE] make a union. They didn't make the union. I went back to New York. In New York, I find a [INAUDIBLE]. I know I'll know what to do and with the [INAUDIBLE]. I know experience. I went to [INAUDIBLE] company. And I worked with [INAUDIBLE] in front from the table. And they gave me also [INAUDIBLE] $70 a week. But [INAUDIBLE] to-- to the work is two hours travelling. I work an [? hour. ?] Then I went to the union in 1953. The union gave me a job. And I work since then. And I make [INAUDIBLE]. I make [INAUDIBLE]. I know everyone. Because I stayed in contact with them. I know them. And this is-- my wife said, listen, you have to get used to it. And that's it. So [INAUDIBLE] where ever you want. And I work there. And then I came back to New York. And I work for-- they called it [? City ?] Kosher-- Hebrew National in the beginning. Then I went to [? City ?] Kosher, a small place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked there seven years there, 1953 to 1960. And then, working there, I says, maybe you cannot make a-- I say at that time, $15,000 [INAUDIBLE]. 15 is [INAUDIBLE]. I got a-- you want to see the records? There are the records. I bought a house. I got a-- when I bought the house, yeah, I took the house. I bought [LAUGHS] in my name. I come in here. About my daughter and my wife's name? He says, it cost you another $200.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have a wife. You have a child. And you didn't tell me. And I make everything correct. It cost you a lot of money. But I'll take your $125 to change [INAUDIBLE] you, and your wife, and your child. Then I have the copies are there. [INAUDIBLE] house and the second house, another-- this way. And I start to make money. And my children, they have to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of your two sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edwin and Robert. Now, it's Robert. Then you have to go to school. The older one, has 86 average. [INAUDIBLE] 86 average. Then he should go in the army. In the American army, you could go. But you have to go to school. And the other, 96 average. It was the top from the city. It's top from the school. He could go to [INAUDIBLE]. He could go any place he wanted. I think they went to-- went to Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Europe, to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went back to-- I been back to-- to Lodz? No. No. No. I didn't go back. I'll [INAUDIBLE] nothing do. But from Poland, I went back to my place blood. I went [? there ?] [? by ?] the Russians, they give me a statement. And I-- 10 days to go back in the Russian in the Polish to looking for families. At time, I was down there. I went three days. And I was looking to me [INAUDIBLE] bandits. They're looking for me. They give $500 in order to catch me, this and the doctor there, which I [INAUDIBLE] with me. Dr. [INAUDIBLE] over here, [INAUDIBLE] call-- call him up right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I run away middle of the night. I went to the station. I come to the station, three, four people, hand up. What's this, 1 o'clock?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the [INAUDIBLE] bandits come and go also to them. I says, I'm Jewish and told [INAUDIBLE] the office and tell them who is this. I go-- I go to-- back to Poland-- to Polish country.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you can't go back, because she's danger. You go back to Luck, L-U-C-K. From there, you take right away train where ever you want to go. I come to Luck. He says, how do you come over here? When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck, L-U-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, when? When was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. No, let's go back to where we were. We were talking--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee

speaker: interviewer

text: Which, I don't [? need ?] to talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] bended down. He lost a piece. I says, what are you going to to do with your hat? He bent up, he broke-- he broke off the table. He said, the way he talks is funny. Grandpa talks funny. [INAUDIBLE]. He likes-- Did you ever talk to your sons about what you want through during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I tell them, too. But they say, you have to forget about it. We don't want to know. They listen to it. Says-- says, thing which is over. That's it. Because there it is. I can't forget about my brother, which I [INAUDIBLE] the binding from the brother comes not from birth. It comes from living-- live together. I give them the $200. I give him everything. And I saved him. And just one second in January 1944, we got this guy over here. I'll call him up, [INAUDIBLE]. He's alive [INAUDIBLE] 10, and I am alive. That is the result of that. He would live, he would find me.

text: Did your-- did your feelings about God change as a result of what you saw during the war?

text: What change?

text: The way you felt about God.

text: God is a business I don't-- I don't bother with that. I tell you what. Didn't I tell you that 1939 when the Russian occupied what [INAUDIBLE] belonged, [INAUDIBLE] neutral. And now I come to America. Democrat, Republican, what ever they say, I say I'm neutral. [INAUDIBLE] say. Which I find out in the practice, I don't belong here. I don't belong there. But changes comes, they're checking for it. And they took all the-- the people that belonged to the Communist Party they killed. And the people where they are-- the Polish, you know, is no good. Religious, they don't like it. Then I don't belong to anybody. I go to Israel. I go to the synagogue. And today also, I have to put a hat, I put here, I put there. I stay with [INAUDIBLE] to get the people. And that's [INAUDIBLE] raise up. When I was young, I had my friends Polacks, Ukrainish. I used to go sometimes to the church with them. The-- it comes in. I give him a zloty. I never forget. And I-- they knew my [INAUDIBLE]. And I come to the synagogue. There's also-- they are-- respect it nicely. They like it. We talk about Bible. They learn about Bible. And that what teaching today, same thing, best [INAUDIBLE], lot of luck. I like [INAUDIBLE]. When I have to [INAUDIBLE], I go with the [INAUDIBLE]. I know don't think about. But let me stop, one thing I must to tell you. My father came from this-- came home. I was about 12, 13 years old. And I learn [NON-ENGLISH]. This [INAUDIBLE] how to make the-- [NON-ENGLISH], preparation about tefillin. And then I always talk about how all things till today I should come to the synagogue. They offered me a job again with the tefillin. I should teach it. My friend is a teacher [? till. ?] And I know better than him the story. I don't want to make money from that business. I keep it's a business. Kosher [INAUDIBLE] business. [INAUDIBLE], kosher, in my knowledge was-- kosher was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we could call it years ago [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of the Jewish Orthodox, Jewish sanitary law, kosher. They call it kosher. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] All the meanings in between them, kosher is it. Anyway, that kosher today turns out-- it's kosher is [? bred ?] for the quality. Kosher [? are ?] [? bred ?] for the quality, are quality kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could be a goy is kosher. You ever see a goy eats kosher? A goy eats kosher. Kosher is the watchman. We is a watchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: A goy likes kosher, because she watches. After three days, you're not allowed to eat it. It's only kosher you could see, not other thing. Not [INAUDIBLE] you couldn't see.

speaker: interviewee

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took over, was the [INAUDIBLE] there. [INAUDIBLE]. There's no more killing. But the Jewish-- the Jewish police, they watch it anyway. The Ukrainish police watch it, nobody should make business. This I want to know. I cannot find it out. I don't remember. I had two children. He had two-- the other guy had two children. The other is not Jewish for sure. But he was saved me. And I want to know if he asked-- only thing I had for me, I gave faxes to work out for them. And I gave them back the receipt. It was 19-- September 19-- in the beginning of 8 of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to me. He took away the receipt. If he has-- If he has the faxes-- a couple fax in a row. You have to [INAUDIBLE]. You understand? This is I'm interested to know. And I don't-- I don't want to open up that story. I don't want to-- very possible, he's living. This guy told me, he's living [INAUDIBLE]. See?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then this I'm very interested to know. Because the [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE]. One, the two that saved my life, and that saved the city, which nobody-- only I know it. This guy was with me together. Nobody-- the city didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why didn't the city know? Because if they would know we had a [INAUDIBLE] in the city, yeah, then that story, the last Saturday in 1942, I was delivering the letter. Saturday, I was delivering the letter in 1942. And the-- but the manager, [INAUDIBLE] Grinkler-- Winkler was not there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the manager in the corner from the business outside the ghetto-- not out-- in the ghetto, outside the ghetto he had an office. And I was eating lunch down there. I could go any place. I had [INAUDIBLE]. But I could go any place I wanted. Nobody. I could go. I had lunch down there. His family's over here. [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [INAUDIBLE] Zimmerman, too. And the wife and the whole family, we eat lunch together, have a good lunch like a normal-- normal table, a big table. It was outside the ghetto. And I told him the story which we have-- like to you. But nobody should know it. I told you the story which we have. [INAUDIBLE], he says to me, we have a Winkler is very good. And when I changed the name for Winkler, the guy says Winkler. And he sees me, and he says-- to him-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Something is there. Because I look this guy just like same [INAUDIBLE] other one. Says, they brothers. He's told them another story. He told me the story. He says, he keeps quiet. He said, don't say anything. I told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then Winkler says to him, don't remind anything I told you about it. Forget about it. See that? OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] That's finished?

text: No, that's Winkler. Now, the story, which I've been telling you about this. I was very interested to know when you're gonna see in the paper, you're gonna have advertised in the German papers, he's going to talk. If he's living, [INAUDIBLE] Winkler, [INAUDIBLE]. He remembers exactly. Because he remembers they have the guy [INAUDIBLE]. This guy, they keep seven days in the city. And they pulled him around. All over, he went to the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy is very-- he could run away probably. If I would be, I would run away. [INAUDIBLE] here there. Why they keep him seven day? He showed [INAUDIBLE], he shows [INAUDIBLE]. He [INAUDIBLE]. He [INAUDIBLE]. Show him everything. Thank you.

You get a picture down there. I've been using everything in Poland. Once it happened, I've been using this. The [INAUDIBLE] in Poland Lodz, Krakow, I was going to [INAUDIBLE] Jewish. Then I showed this over here. The other guy says in Polish, says I can't speak Polish. They took them off. And I says, over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Says-- he says one to the other, see? I told you this is a Russian. And I took off 15 Jewish people. And I have this here. It saved my life, Krakow, Lodz. OK.
